narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know it's disheartening to hear that your law school application was rejected based on our model's assessment of your bar exam prospects, predicting a 40% chance of not passing—a risk that's just too high for us to endorse right now. Picture the model looking at your journey like a story unfolding over time: it starts with your Year 1 grades placing you in the 4th decile at school, which was the third strongest signal pulling toward this tougher outlook, suggesting an early struggle to stand out among peers. But what weighed heaviest was your Year 3 decile of 3rd, the top influence tipping the scales toward failure, as if even with time, the upward momentum didn't quite take hold. Your LSAT score of 32 came in as the second biggest factor, ranking lower than what the model sees in those who typically succeed, painting a picture of foundational challenges in the high-stakes reasoning the bar demands. Layered on that, your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 added another nudge toward risk, reinforcing a pattern of solid but not standout academic performance. Your middle family income bracket played a supporting role here too, the fifth most influential piece, hinting at fewer resources that often buffer students through intense prep. Even elements like being a white female pursuing full-time studies and your clear passion for law (that target_law drive) couldn't fully counterbalance these, as they each subtly aligned with the model's concerns drawn from patterns in past students. It's not that you're not capable or committed—far from it—but together, these pieces formed a narrative of higher hurdles ahead, much like stories we've seen before where similar profiles faced the bar and faltered. The good news? This isn't set in stone; with targeted boosts like LSAT retakes or grade improvements, you could rewrite that story. We're rooting for you to find the right path forward.
